TextENGNew: Originally, Miyo-san and I didn't have much of a connection.
We were acquainted enough to exchange a polite nod if we passed on the street.@k ...That was the extent of our relationship.
In truth, we'd only ever had one real conversation.
...And that was quite recently.
A little before the Watanagashi festival.
changeReason: Timing corrected: 'between the day of the Watanagashi festival and...' to 'A little before the Watanagashi festival' (JPN 少し前 means 'a little before').

TextENGNew: It was a few days after I'd killed Rina and the others, and Keiichi-kun and my friends had discovered what I'd done.
changeReason: Old says 'caught in the act of burying' but Japanese describes killing then being discovered days later.

TextENGNew: That evening, the library called to let us know that my father had forgotten to return a book he'd borrowed.
Since Dad had gone out shopping at the home improvement store, I decided to return it for him.
While I was there, I figured I might as well borrow something for myself, and wandered idly among the shelves.@k ...That's where I ran into her.
``My, isn't that Rena-chan?@k@vS09/09/170900002. Hello.``
``Miyo-san.@k@vS09/02/170200618. Hello.@k@vS09/02/170200618_1. What a surprise to run into you at the library.``
``Oh?@k@vS09/09/170900005. From my perspective, it's far more unusual to see you here. I'm always at this library.@k@vS09/09/170900006. Hee hee.``
Her expression carried that alluring composure that only a mature woman could possess.@k ...There was no hint in it that her own death awaited her just days away.
She told me she often met Tomitake-san at this library.
Perhaps to kill some time while waiting for him, she offered to buy me a drink.
I had no particular reason to rush home.
She treated me to a canned iced milk tea from the vending machine inside the library, and for a while we chatted freely.
...Looking back on it now, there was a bitter irony to that conversation.
Because she herself had brought up the Hinamizawa serial mysterious deaths.
Not knowing that she would soon be counted among the fifth year's victims.
``...Serial... mysterious deaths...?``
``Oh, you must have heard of it at least.``

``Four years ago, it was the dam construction site foreman.@k@vS09/09/170900009. Three years ago, the Houjou couple.@k@vS09/09/170900010. Two years ago, the Furude couple.@k@vS09/09/170900011. And last year, Satoko-chan's aunt became the victim. That string of bizarre incidents they call 'Oyashiro-sama's curse.'``
``I... I know it's referred to by that ominous name, yes.@k@vS09/02/170200621. ...But that doesn't mean a fifth year has to happen...``

``It will happen.@k@vS09/09/170900014. ...Without question.@k@vS09/09/170900015. There's a saying that what happens twice will happen thrice, but wouldn't you agree that what's happened four times is even more likely to happen a fifth?``
The very person who was so convinced the fifth year's incident would occur became its victim herself...@k You could call it irony, or an eerie coincidence.
But there had always been something unsettling about her.@k Even if she'd known she would be killed in a matter of days, I felt she would have worn that same enigmatic smile.
The Hinamizawa serial mysterious deaths.
...Of course, having lived in Hinamizawa for over a year, there was no way I didn't know about it.
Every year on the day of the Watanagashi festival, enemies of the village dating back to the dam war met mysterious deaths, one after another.
Each individual case had been more or less resolved on its own, and yet... the bizarre incidents continued, year after year.
There seemed to be some kind of will behind it all, yet at the same time, perhaps not.
...The incomprehensible eeriness of it all certainly warranted the name 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama.'
...In truth, I had always believed that 'the curse of Oyashiro-sama' was nothing more than a string of coincidences.
My basis for that was a strong conviction that the fourth year's incident—the bludgeoning murder of the aunt—was Satoshi-kun's doing.
I had no evidence whatsoever, but... I was certain of it.
I knew all too well how cornered Satoshi-kun had been last year, how utterly without escape.
No one would help him, yet he had to save Satoko-chan, and urgently.
...Just as I had done with Rina and the others, Satoshi-kun must have agonized over it and ultimately chosen what he saw as the 'best option.' I couldn't imagine any other explanation.
But I could hardly announce that Satoshi-kun was the fourth year's culprit, so for a while I simply listened to what Miyo-san had to say.
According to Miyo-san's theory, the serial mysterious deaths were not a mere accumulation of coincidences but a genuine connected series of crimes.
This put her theory in direct opposition to mine, but I decided to hear her out without interrupting.
``You know about the Oyashiro-sama faith here in Hinamizawa, don't you?``
Takano-san began by explaining that Hinamizawa was once called Onigafuchi.

She went on to tell me about the ancient villagers who were feared as man-eating demons yet simultaneously revered as sages, illustrating it all with her prized scrapbook, explaining everything in meticulous detail.

``...So according to your theory,@k@vS09/02/170200623. there's a devout group hidden in Hinamizawa that worships Oyashiro-sama, and they're trying to demonstrate Oyashiro-sama's power to the world... Is that what you're saying?``
changeReason: Term changed: 'cultists who worship' to 'a devout group...that worships'; JPN 信心深い団体 means 'devout/pious group', which is more neutral than 'cultists'.

TextENGNew: ``Whether you call them devout or fundamentalist is a fine line.@k@vS09/09/170900018. Since the Meiji era, when the village's name changed to Hinamizawa, many of the customs Onigafuchi had maintained for so long have died out.``
``I think there are people who want to revive those customs and reclaim the name and the awe that Onigafuchi once commanded.``
Miyo-san's theory was outlandish, but it was stimulating and fascinating.
At first I'd dismissed it as an enormous leap, but when she presented it alongside the materials she'd so painstakingly assembled, I began to think it wasn't so far-fetched after all.

But if that were true, then the second year's 'accidental' deaths of the Houjou couple, the third year's illness and suicide of the Furude couple, and the fourth year's fabricated culprit would all require the involvement of a force with powerful influence, reaching even into the police.
When I pointed this out, Miyo-san nodded and began explaining just how deeply 'they' were rooted in the community, and how far their reach extended into various public institutions.
By the time the conversation had gone this far, I finally realized that Miyo-san was suggesting the Sonozaki family and the other so-called Three Great Families of Hinamizawa were the masterminds.
It's true... I know that Mii-chan's Sonozaki family wields tremendous power.
Their influence spans not just Hinamizawa and Okinomiya, but the entire city of Shishibone.
No—it wouldn't be wrong to say it extends across the entire prefecture.
If the Sonozaki family had been pulling strings behind the scenes all along... then yes, it wasn't inconceivable.
But... that would mean suspecting Mii-chan.@k ...No, I shouldn't.@k ...And yet, when Satoshi-kun was suffering, Mii-chan had been unimaginably cold—nothing like her usual self.
...I knew there had been a feud between the Sonozaki and Houjou families during the dam war, but... even so, she'd been terribly cold.
But Mii-chan had acknowledged that sin.
And I had forgiven her.
So I didn't want to think about it anymore...
Sensing that any further discussion implicating the Sonozaki family would only make me uncomfortable, I decided to steer the conversation elsewhere.

``In the first place, what exactly is the Oyashiro-sama faith all about...?@k@vS09/02/170200625. The idea of demons and humans coexisting is fascinating.@k@vS09/02/170200626. In most old stories, when demons appear, people defeat them—that's how it goes.``

``That's true.@k@vS09/09/170900021. That's what makes the Oyashiro-sama faith so interesting.@k@vS09/09/170900022. But actually, it's not uncommon at all for a god to drift ashore from the divine realm and be worshipped.``
``It's called drifted-god worship.@k@vS09/09/170900024. All around the world, there are myths about gods arriving from across the sea, or from upriver, and being revered by the people.``
``...Would that be... the theory of foreign castaways?``
``That interpretation is the common one, yes.@k@vS09/09/170900026. In ancient times, Japan lagged behind the continent in many aspects of culture.``
``So when a foreigner with advanced knowledge washed ashore, the people revered that knowledge and enshrined them as a god... It's not an unreasonable theory.``
``Come to think of it, there's a theory that the red oni and blue oni of Japanese folklore actually represent foreigners who were shipwrecked and drifted to shore.``
``And the tengu—with that bright red face and impossibly long nose—might have been created by ancient people who had never seen Westerners before.``

``My, Rena-chan, you have quite a sharp eye.@k@vS09/09/170900029. I agree with you entirely.@k@vS09/09/170900030. I believe most instances of drifted-god worship do originate from foreign castaways.``
``But if that's the case, there's a rather curious problem with Onigafuchi's drifted-god worship—the claim that 'demons appeared.'@k@vS09/09/170900032. ...Can you see what it is?``
``...I'm afraid I can't.``
``It's simple.@k@vS09/09/170900034. Onigafuchi has no sea.``
``Ah... you're right.``

``For a foreign castaway, there has to be some point of contact with the outside world.@k@vS09/09/170900036. In Europe, that would be a river connecting to foreign lands.@k@vS09/09/170900037. In Japan, it would be the sea, which faces the wider world.@k@vS09/09/170900038. But Onigafuchi is a mountain village, deep in the interior. There's no sea, and no great river either.``
``Isn't it possible the foreigner washed ashore somewhere else and then traveled here on foot?``
``The critical point about the Oyashiro-sama faith, though, is that@k@vS09/09/170900040. ...it specifically identifies Onigafuchi Swamp as the place where the demons 'drifted ashore.'``

``Nothing washes up in Onigafuchi Swamp.@k@vS09/09/170900042. So why, and from where, did something 'drift' there?@k@vS09/09/170900043. ...This is actually where the real fascination of studying the Oyashiro-sama faith lies.``
``How much do you know about the legend of Oyashiro-sama?``
``Only what I read in the picture book at school.``
I felt like I knew the story well enough, but in reality, my knowledge wasn't all that deep.
...What I did have was an overwhelming fear of the particular 'rule' that forbade leaving Hinamizawa.
...That was because I genuinely believed that my life had gone wrong after I left Hinamizawa...
...Thinking any further made my head go hazy and foggy, so I stopped.
At the very end of my time in Ibaraki, when I'd wrecked everything... I met Oyashiro-sama, and he told me to return to Hinamizawa... ...didn't he??
No, no... my head is getting cloudy...@k Don't remember, don't remember. I can't remember, I can't remember...

I'd simply become self-destructive because I blamed myself for my parents' divorce.@k I'd just pinned my mental instability on Oyashiro-sama and cried that I wanted to go back to Hinamizawa... wasn't that all it was...?
But wait, that would mean...@k But Oyashiro-sama really did come to me...@k No, it's too bright.@k ...All I can recall is those garish capsules and the foggy sensation in my head...
``That's more than enough.@k@vS09/09/170900046. ...Demons poured forth from the Onigafuchi and attacked the villagers.``

``Oyashiro-sama, unable to stand by and watch, descended and brought the demons to their knees.@k@vS09/09/170900048. The demons, banished from the underworld and lamenting that they had nowhere left to go,@k@vS09/09/170900049. moved the villagers to pity, and so the villagers took them in.``

``And Oyashiro-sama, moved by the villagers' compassion, granted the demons human form and allowed them to coexist in the village.@k@vS09/09/170900051. Over time, their blood intermingled and they blended in among the villagers.``
changeReason: Pronoun changed: 'he gave human forms' to 'granted the demons human form' (removing gendered pronoun); JPN does not assign a gender to Oyashiro-sama.

TextENGNew: ``...Yes, that's the story as I remember it.``
Having brushed against memories I'd resolved not to revisit, I felt something like anemia wash over me.
``Many myths are said to be rooted in some ancient truth.@k@vS09/09/170900053. ...What do you think this myth is telling us, Rena-chan?``
I was still somewhat dazed, and could manage nothing more than a puzzled tilt of my head.
Miyo-san seemed to have expected that I wouldn't have an immediate answer; she smiled with satisfaction at seeing me stumped.
Then she rummaged through her paper bag and pulled out yet another scrapbook.
``Here, I'll lend this one to you, Rena-chan.``

``Don't worry about it. Just read it whenever you have a quiet evening to spare.@k@vS09/09/170900056. You seem to have a sharp mind, Rena-chan, so after you've read it, I'd love to have a proper discussion with you.``
``I've been lonely without someone to debate this with, whether they agree with me or oppose me.@k@vS09/09/170900058. ...Jirou-san has absolutely no interest in this sort of thing.``
Put that way, I could hardly refuse to take the scrapbook.
...Miyo-san seemed to frequent the library, so I figured I could return it to her the next time I came to bring back the books I'd borrowed today.
Ironically, the day for returning that scrapbook to Miyo-san would never come...
``What's wrong?@k@vS09/09/170900060. You've seemed distant for a while now.@k@vS09/09/170900061. Are you feeling all right?``
``Oh... I might be coming down with a summer cold.@k@vS09/02/170200639. ...I'm still not fully recovered.``
It wasn't the summer cold that was making me zone out.
...It was because whenever I tried to remember meeting Oyashiro-sama in Ibaraki, my mind would invariably go blank.
I... summoned my courage and decided to ask her.
...Those final jumbled memories from Ibaraki.
...The vision of Oyashiro-sama that I was certain I'd seen.
No matter who I told, they all treated it as my delusion.
But I thought Miyo-san—she alone might listen to me seriously.
``Miyo-san.@k@vS09/02/170200641. ...Um... would it be all right if I told you something?``
``Of course.@k@vS09/09/170900063. You listened to me, so now it's my turn to listen to you.``
``Well... it's about Oyashiro-sama.@k@vS09/02/170200643. ...I'm... really afraid you might laugh at me.``
``I won't laugh.@k@vS09/09/170900065. You didn't laugh at me, after all.``
Miyo-san said it gently, with the kind of warm, embracing assurance that only an adult could offer.
Even so, I nearly lost my nerve... but there was probably no one else in the world I could tell this to besides Miyo-san.
After swallowing my words several times, I finally mustered my courage.
``I... have met... Oyashiro-sama.``
``...Really?``

``Yes.@k@vS09/02/170200646. ...I lived in Hinamizawa until just before I started elementary school.@k@vS09/02/170200647. But then, because of my mother's work, we moved to Ibaraki.@k@vS09/02/170200648. ...I think you can understand what I'm trying to say.``
``I do.@k@vS09/09/170900068. ...You broke Oyashiro-sama's taboo—the prohibition against leaving Hinamizawa.``

``That's right.@k@vS09/02/170200650. And then... all sorts of terrible things happened. My mother divorced my father... and our family fell apart.@k@vS09/02/170200651. I became self-destructive and did terrible things.``
``...You went through so much.@k@vS09/09/170900070. ...And then?``
``I came to hate everything in the world, and near the end, I felt like every happy person was looking down on me.@k@vS09/02/170200653. ...That's when something strange began to appear.``
``That was... Oyashiro-sama?``
``The first time it appeared was... um, please keep this a secret.@k@vS09/02/170200655. ...I'd given up on everything, and I wanted to destroy myself, so... I held a blade to my own body.``
``...When I cut my neck with the razor... mixed in with the blood, something foul came bubbling out.@k@vS09/02/170200657. It was... it was... nn...``
``...I'm not going to make fun of you.@k@vS09/09/170900073. Go on.``
The chill and revulsion I'd felt that day began crawling up my spine.
A sealed memory I'd locked away, vowing never to recall.
...But once the seal was broken, the rest came flooding back in a chain.
Yes, that time, mixed in with the blood... they were there.

``Dark, reddish-black... countless somethings...!@k@vS09/02/170200659. They came swarming out of my neck along with the blood.@k@vS09/02/170200660. And what's worse, they were trying to crawl back inside through the wound before they could spill out!``

``...I didn't want those revolting things going back inside my body... so I clawed at it. I clawed at the wound on my neck, trying to dig them out.@k@vS09/02/170200662. But the more I did, the more it hurt, and I just got covered in blood...``
``And then I suddenly became terrified.@k@vS09/02/170200663. What if my body was packed full of those disgusting things... filling every blood vessel?!``

``So I... as a test, I opened a blood vessel on my thigh with the razor. I thought I could get a better look there.@k@vS09/02/170200665. And sure enough... they came pouring out!! Gushing, swarming!!``
All right, all right, we understand, Ryuugu-san. Calm down, please calm down...

``You don't understand!@k@vS09/02/170200667. They come gushing out with the blood, but they immediately try to crawl back into the blood vessel!!@k@vS09/02/170200668. And it itches so badly!!@k@vS09/02/170200669. It itches, it itches, it itches!!``
Someone, fetch a doctor, quickly!@k No, no, no—stop clawing at yourself!!@k A sedative!@k Hurry!!@k Hold her down!
``Kill the things inside my veins!!@k@vS09/02/170200671. Drain all the blood out and clean me!!@k@vS09/02/170200672. My blood vessels are crammed full of those things!!``
``Why won't you believe me?!@k@vS09/02/170200674. I'll show you!@k@vS09/02/170200675. You'll believe me when you see them, won't you?!@k@vS09/02/170200676. No more injections, no no no no no!!``
``Calm down.@k@vS09/09/170900075. ...There, there.``
Miyo-san had sat down beside me and was squeezing my hand tightly.@k ...The warmth of her grip brought me back to myself.
``...And then... what happened?``
``When no one would believe me... that's when it appeared.@k@vS09/02/170200678. ...Oyashiro-sama.``
...The memory was hazy, blindingly bright and prickling, painful to recall...
'I met Oyashiro-sama'—that single statement was all I retained.
The actual memory of meeting Oyashiro-sama... was in truth unbelievably vague.

Even after being discharged from the hospital and convalescing at home, I would still occasionally feel something stirring in my veins, and I'd want to split them open, let the blood flow, and check whether anything was mixed in.
Most of the time, the powerful medication kept me in a death-like sleep,@k ...but on that particular day, I'd happened to miss my dose... and my mind was perfectly clear.
So I remembered the 'somethings' swarming in my blood vessels, and a sudden wave of anxiety hit me, and I desperately wanted to look inside.
And so, slowly, I drew a razor along the vein visible on my wrist...
changeReason: Original heavily truncated; new translation restores the method detail (drawing a razor along the vein, JPN カミソリを入れて) and the slow, deliberate nature of the act.

TextENGNew: And from it... just as I'd feared, blood and masses of dark, reddish-black, filthy things, so many of them...!!
I wanted to wash them away before they could burrow back into the wound, so I held my wrist under running water in the bathroom, trying to keep the vein open.
Water and blood and... 'those things'... splashing down into the drain.
...As if squeezing out the contents of a pimple... I crushed and wrung out my vein, forcing the dirty blood and those things out, again and again.
On and on and on...!!
Even as my arm went numb and I began to feel strangely cold, I endured it and kept going, on and on and on!!
...Even when I thought I might die from losing too much blood... on and on and on!!
Because my entire body was filled with those things—I had to squeeze out every last drop of blood to get rid of them all!!
That was when... a blinding light filled everything.
It was... a mystical experience unlike anything I'd ever known.
The dim bathroom was suddenly flooded with radiant light.
...It was dazzling yet gentle—nothing like the merciless glare of a light bulb or fluorescent lamp.@k ...It was an impossibly warm light, overflowing with tenderness.
That was... the descent of Oyashiro-sama.
Oyashiro-sama gazed at me... with eyes that were gentle, yet filled with deep sorrow.
And when he softly touched my wound... those things that no amount of washing could remove melted away before my eyes and vanished.
More than that—even the things writhing through every vein in my body, tickling my insides, dissolved as if they'd never been...!
Oyashiro-sama told me.
...That these strange creatures were the manifestation of the curse.
changeReason: Object changed: 'maggots' to 'strange creatures' (JPN 変な物体 = 'strange objects/entities', not specifically maggots); also 'were the curse' vs 'manifestation of the curse' adds nuance.

TextENGNew: Those born in Hinamizawa who carry the blood of the demons must not live apart from Hinamizawa.
This is the decree, he said.
You have been visited by the curse, and for that I am sorry.
...It is a sad and pitiable thing.@k But there is one way to be saved... That is what he told me.
What must I do to be saved?!
Oyashiro-sama's answer was perfectly clear.
Return to Hinamizawa.
Even before I heard it from Oyashiro-sama... I had already understood this, deep down.
We who were born in Hinamizawa must never leave.
I had broken Oyashiro-sama's law by moving away, and that's why... my family fell apart, and I fell apart with it...!
If we had never left Hinamizawa, nothing would have gone wrong!
That's right—I have to go back...!
Oyashiro-sama also said this:
He could make these maggots—the embodiment of the curse—disappear, but so far from Hinamizawa, the effect would be only temporary.
In time, Oyashiro-sama's power would fade, and the curse would begin to gnaw at my body once more.
...When that happened, everything inside me would be taken over by something else, and eventually I would have no choice but to perish...
Return to Hinamizawa... before the power holding the curse at bay fades away entirely.
That is what Oyashiro-sama told me.
Yes, he said it, he definitely said it—I heard it with my own ears!
I should have gone back to Hinamizawa far, far sooner.@k That's why Oyashiro-sama came for me, why he sent the curse...!!
But Oyashiro-sama gave me a chance.@k He gave me a chance to atone for the sin of leaving Hinamizawa!!
Dad, listen—Oyashiro-sama will save us.@k Let's go back to Hinamizawa. Hinamizawa is where we belong.@k If we don't, it won't just be me—the curse might come for you too!!
Let's go back, let's go back to Hinamizawa—it's the only land where we're meant to live!
Hurry, hurry!@k If we don't go back soon... Oyashiro-sama's power will fade, and those things will take over my body again...!!@k Aah, Oyashiro-sama is following me, whispering at me to hurry, hurry!
Following me everywhere, urging me to hurry!@k Apologizing again and again for having to send the curse, I'm sorry, I'm sorry.@k Following me always, everywhere, apologizing endlessly!!
It's true, you know?!@k I'm telling the truth!!@k Why can't any of you see it?!@k Why can't you see these things, or Oyashiro-sama?!
You're all conspiring to deceive me—you can all see them too! No, no, stop it, please no more injections...!!!
``...Have you... calmed down...?``
...Before I knew it, I was breathing raggedly, and Miyo-san had her arm around my shoulders.
...I could feel those loathsome memories—the ones I'd managed to forget until today—stirring back to life.
The doctors had told me... that memory was merely my delusion.

...They said that because I blamed myself too much for the divorce, I'd unconsciously sought to place that blame on something else, and my subconscious had latched onto the Oyashiro-sama folktale and its taboos—stories that had frightened me as a small child.
Medication and counseling... and time.
Through all of that, I gradually calmed down... and began to accept that what I'd experienced had been my own delusion.
The doctors had apparently suggested to my father that he consider moving to Hinamizawa, if at all possible.
Fortunately, my father was also still unable to heal from the pain of the divorce and wanted a complete change of environment.
And so... we returned to Hinamizawa.
When we came back to Hinamizawa... I felt it, unmistakably.
This was the land where we truly belonged.
And I felt it with certainty.@k Oyashiro-sama's blessing.@k I felt it beyond any doubt.@k The curse of Oyashiro-sama dissolving away.@k I was... set free...!!
Even after moving, I continued going to the psychosomatic medicine clinic in Gogura for a while.
Those garish capsules were prescribed for me here as well, and I was made to keep taking them.
Gradually... the loathsome memories grew indistinct,@k ...and whenever I tried to recall them, all I could retrieve was the heavy, clouding side effects of the medication... and I regained my peace.
And so, I discarded the name Reina.
Being called by that name felt as though it would dredge up everything I didn't want to remember.
I'd had enough of unpleasant things... So I decided to remove the 'unpleasant' from my life.
When I took the 'i' of 'iya'—the Japanese word for unpleasant—out of Reina, I was left with 'Re' and 'na.'
Put them together and you get Rena.@k ...It sounded kind of cute.
So when I introduced myself at school, I asked everyone to call me Rena.
From that day on, I've been Rena.
Living a new life as Rena.
Leaving behind all the painful things that happened when I was Reina.
My father still calls me Reina, but being called that by family didn't bother me so much.
And then along came someone who wasn't family yet called me Reina all the same... that woman, Rina.
Because she kept calling me Reina... the flickering, the flickering, flickering, flickering...@k ...I should stop. I should forget...@k Flickering, flickering, flickering...
``...I'm sorry.@k@vS09/02/170200680. ...Was I... losing it badly just now...?``
``You were whispering something to yourself.``
``But I'm glad you've calmed down.``
Judging by Miyo-san's reaction... I hadn't lost control as badly as I'd feared.
But... I may have been slipping in and out of those panicked episodes multiple times.
...If I'd started screaming and raving like I once did... they would surely have carted me off to the hospital again.
``In the midst of all that madness, I truly did meet Oyashiro-sama.@k@vS09/02/170200682. And those horrifying 'somethings,' too.@k@vS09/02/170200683. ...Ahaha... you don't believe me, do you...?``
Miyo-san wore a complicated smile.
...Of course. Even I wouldn't expect anyone to believe a story like that.
...I was probably just delusional, fabricating strange memories in my confusion.@k ...How wretched, to be unable to trust your own self.@k ...It was the worst feeling imaginable.
``Rena-chan.@k@vS09/09/170900082. First, there's one thing I want you to feel reassured about.``

``The curse you're so afraid of.@k@vS09/09/170900084. ...In other words, the strange things you saw in your blood.@k@vS09/09/170900085. According to what Oyashiro-sama told you, as long as you stay in Hinamizawa, you're safe, right?``
``...Yes, that's what he said...``
``Then there's nothing to be afraid of.@k@vS09/09/170900087. You can relax.@k@vS09/09/170900088. You're in Hinamizawa right now.``

``Well, technically this library is in Okinomiya, but I'm sure Oyashiro-sama would forgive this much distance.@k@vS09/09/170900090. You don't feel anything stirring inside your veins right now, do you?``
``So relax.@k@vS09/09/170900092. You're properly following Oyashiro-sama's laws right now.@k@vS09/09/170900093. So there's nothing to fear.@k@vS09/09/170900094. After all, isn't that right?``
I had never felt such warmth and acceptance from Miyo-san before.
Every doctor I'd ever told this story to had invariably ignored me, brushed me off, or reached for that hateful needle.
...And yet Miyo-san never dismissed me outright, never denied the existence of Oyashiro-sama.
People in the village sometimes whispered that she was... a little strange,@k ...but perhaps she was truly a kind person at heart.@k ...That's what I felt.
``Rena-chan.@k@vS09/09/170900097. I believe every word of what you just told me.``
``...Thank you.@k@vS09/02/170200690. You might be the first person who's ever said that to me...``
``Hee hee, the feeling is mutual.@k@vS09/09/170900099. You're the first person who's ever listened to me without making fun of me.@k@vS09/09/170900100. ...I think we could become very good friends.``
Miyo-san said that, then leaned her forehead against mine and smiled.
``In that case... I'll lend you my most prized files and scrapbooks.``

``These are truly, absolutely confidential—never meant to leave my possession.@k@vS09/09/170900103. I never intended to show them to anyone, but for you, Rena-chan, I feel I can make an exception.@k@vS09/09/170900104. ...I trust you to take them seriously.``
With that, she pulled two or three more scrapbooks from her paper bag and pressed them into my hands.
``Please read them and let me know what you think.@k@vS09/09/170900106. ...Or perhaps... there are things in there that only someone like you, Rena-chan, could understand.``
Miyo-san said something faintly cryptic.
Then she flashed a mischievous smile and raised her index finger to her lips in a gesture of secrecy.
``And keep these scrapbooks—and the fact that I'm conducting this kind of research—a secret, would you?``
``A secret?@k@vS09/02/170200692. Yes, I understand.``
``...Because if they found out I was doing this kind of research, they might silence me.@k@vS09/09/170900109. ...Hee hee hee hee.``
She laughed as she said it.
If they found out, they might silence her.
That's what she said, and she laughed.
And then... she vanished.
...Leaving me with nothing but a few scrapbooks.
But the shock didn't end there.
It was... the way Tomitake-san had died.
Ooishi-san had described it as a drug-induced suicide in a state of delirium, or something to that effect... but I alone knew the truth behind that death.
...Of course, I hadn't told Ooishi-san.@k ...Because I didn't think he would believe me even if I did.
Tomitake-san had... without a doubt...@k ...died trying to claw those things out of his body.
That was nothing other than the curse of Oyashiro-sama.
The taboos of Oyashiro-sama don't just forbid leaving Hinamizawa.
There are several others.@k ...He may have unknowingly violated one of them.
That's right—Ooishi-san had said it himself, hadn't he?
changeReason: Tone and structure changed: factual 'Ooishi-san told me' to rhetorical self-persuasion 'Ooishi-san had said it himself, hadn't he?' (JPN じゃないか = rhetorical 'isn't that so?').

TextENGNew: ...That Tomitake-san had been taking photographs with his flash during the sacred offering dance, and that it had drawn resentment from some of the villagers.
He had desecrated the sacred dance honoring Oyashiro-sama... and so the curse fell upon him...?
Perhaps for another reason entirely, but... regardless, this was unmistakably the curse of Oyashiro-sama and nothing else.
Which would mean... Miyo-san, too... had fallen to Oyashiro-sama's curse...?
To investigate a god is to approach a god.@k And that is nothing short of blasphemy.
But no, wait... Miyo-san had said that if 'they' found out about her research, they might silence her.
'They' referred to human beings. Not Oyashiro-sama.
...And Miyo-san's theory, if I recalled correctly, was a conspiracy by the Three Great Families to revive the Oyashiro-sama faith.
But the way Tomitake-san died was clearly the work of the curse!
I can say that with absolute certainty—because I've experienced it myself!
And at first glance, that seemed irreconcilable with a theory of human conspiracy.
Wait—hadn't Ooishi-san also mentioned the possibility that a suspicious drug was used to induce the suicide?
Then could it be that... the Three Families possess some secret drug capable of artificially triggering the curse of Oyashiro-sama...?
That couldn't be. Because I had never been given any suspicious injection.
I'd been living an ordinary life in Ibaraki.
I shouldn't have had any connection whatsoever to the Sonozaki family or the Kimiyoshi family or anyone else tied to Hinamizawa...
Ooishi-san had made a request of me.
Mii-chan, Rika-chan, anyone in the village—it didn't matter who.
changeReason: Content swapped with next line: lines 300 and 301 had their English translations exchanged.

TextENGNew: He told me to secretly let him know if I saw or heard anything suspicious.
changeReason: Content swapped with previous line: lines 300 and 301 had their English translations exchanged.

TextENGNew: Miyo-san, who had believed my story about Oyashiro-sama, had been silenced.
Tomitake-san had died doing the very same thing I once did.
No, no, I don't want this...
I don't want anything more to do with the curse of Oyashiro-sama...!
Was this the work of shadowy believers, or the curse of Oyashiro-sama?
Was it all coincidence... or perhaps the conspiracy and the curse were all tangled together?
I don't know, I don't know!@k Human? Curse? Coincidence?!
Even the fading light of the evening sun made me feel dizzy.
When had the sunlight of Hinamizawa become so cruel...?
Miyo-san had told me to keep her research a secret.
...And I was now in possession of those strictly confidential scrapbooks.
...Could it be that... the truth lay sleeping within those pages?
The instant that thought struck me, my heart leapt.
Because if those scrapbooks held the truth... then I, who now possessed them, was in the same danger as Miyo-san.
If it was a conspiracy, I would be silenced.
If it was the curse, I would be cursed.
I should burn the scrapbooks... that would be the safest course...
...But Miyo-san had believed my story.
Miyo-san hadn't made fun of me.
...Could I really burn what she'd left behind without even reading it...?
If it was a conspiracy, it had to be exposed.
If it was the curse, it had to be broken.
I must not run away.@k ...I will fight.
That's right—I had resolved to fight, no matter how far I had to go, until I seized my happiness in my own hands.
Do not yield to misfortune.@k Fight.
...I will dispel with my own hands the dark clouds threatening my peace...!
I'll go home and read Miyo-san's scrapbooks.
My feet quickened of their own accord...
